1: Psalm 109 is the most extended imprecatory psalm, attributed to David. Its central interpretive question: are vv6-20 (1) the Psalmist's curses against his enemies, or (2) a quotation of what the enemies have said against the Psalmist? The singular in v6 (after the plurals of vv2-5) and the shift back in v20 ("let this be the reward of my accusers") support reading (1), but the extended length and specificity of the curses lend plausibility to (2). אֱלֹהֵי תְהִלָּתִי אַל-תֶּחֱרַשׁ (ʾĕlōhê tĕhillātî ʾal-teḥĕrash, "O God — the one I praise — do not be silent!") — ʾĕlōhê tĕhillātî: "God of my praise" — the divine identity declared as the subject of the Psalmist's ongoing praise (Psalms 22:25: "my praise is from you"; Deuteronomy 10:21: "he is your praise"). To address God as "God of my praise" while being attacked by enemies is the psalm's opening paradox: the God who is praised has been silent. ʾAl-teḥĕrash: do not be deaf/silent (Psalms 28:1; Psalms 83:1: the same plea).
2: כִּי פִי רָשָׁע וּפִי-מִרְמָה עָלַי פָּתָחוּ (kî pî rāshāʿ ûpî-mirmāh ʿālay pātāḥû, "For the mouths of the wicked and the treacherous are opened wide against me!") — pî rāshāʿ: the mouth of the wicked (the instrument of the attack — not physical violence but verbal); pî-mirmāh: the mouth of deceit (mirmāh: deception, treachery — Psalms 52:2-4). Two mouths opened against one person: the attack is verbal and coordinated. דִּבְּרוּ אִתִּי לְשׁוֹן שָׁקֶר (dibbĕrû ʾittî lĕshôn shāqer, "they have spoken against me with a lying tongue!") — lĕshôn shāqer: a lying tongue (Proverbs 6:17: one of the seven things YHWH hates). The conflict is fundamentally forensic/verbal: the Psalmist is being accused with false words.
3: וְדִבְרֵי שִׂנְאָה סְבָבוּנִי וַיִּלָּחֲמוּנִי חִנָּם (wĕdibrê sinʾāh sĕbābûnî wayyillāḥăṣûnî ḥinnām, "They encircle me with hateful words and fight against me for no reason!") — dibrê sinʾāh sĕbābûnî: words of hatred have surrounded me (the encirclement of hostile speech — Psalms 22:12,16: "bulls surround me"). Wayyillāḥăṣûnî ḥinnām: they attack/fight against me without cause (ḥinnām: without reason, freely, for nothing — Psalms 35:7,19; Psalms 69:4; John 15:25: "they hated me without reason" quotes the ḥinnām of the Psalms). The groundlessness of the hatred makes it more unbearable and more recognizably satanic in character.
4: תַּחַת-אַהֲבָתִי יִשְׂטְנוּנִי וַאֲנִי תְפִלָּה (taḥat-ʾahăbātî yiśṭĕnûnî waʾănî tĕpillāh, "In exchange for my love they have become my adversaries, though I am prayer itself!") — taḥat-ʾahăbātî: in place of/in return for my love (the return of hatred for love — Psalms 35:12: "they repaid me evil for good"). Yiśṭĕnûnî: they act as my śāṭān — the same root as the legal-court accuser and the cosmic adversary. Waʾănî tĕpillāh: "but I am prayer" — perhaps the most concentrated self-description in the Psalter: the Psalmist does not merely say "I pray"; he says his entire existence is prayer. In response to the verbal attack, prayer is his only posture and his total identity.
5: וַיָּשִׂימוּ עָלַי רָעָה תַּחַת טוֹבָה וְשִׂנְאָה תַּחַת אַהֲבָתִי (wayyāśîmû ʿālay rāʿāh taḥat ṭôbāh wĕsinʾāh taḥat ʾahăbātî, "They have paid me back evil for good and hatred in place of my love!") — the double inversion restated with parallelism: evil for good, hatred for love. The chiastic relationship between love (v4) and hatred (vv4-5) is the psalm's moral center: the Psalmist loved, was attacked; the enemies hated, had been loved. This sets up the question of whether the imprecation that follows is justice or excess. Proverbs 17:13: "if anyone returns evil for good, evil will not depart from their house."
6: הַפְקֵד עָלָיו רָשָׁע וְשָׂטָן יַעֲמֹד עַל-יְמִינוֹ (hapqêd ʿālāyw rāshāʿ wĕśāṭān yaʿămōd ʿal-yĕmînô, "Set a wicked man to judge him; let an accuser take his place at his right hand!") — v6 begins the disputed section (vv6-20). The singular "him" after the plural "they" of vv2-5 suggests either a primary ringleader is targeted or the whole section is a quotation of what the enemies said about the Psalmist. Śāṭān: the legal-court accuser (the noun śāṭān in its pre-apocalyptic meaning: a human or divine adversary who makes accusations — Job 1-2; Zechariah 3:1-2). ʿAl-yĕmînô: at his right hand (the place of the legal advocate — Psalms 16:8; Psalms 110:5; Psalms 109:31 inverts this: God stands at the right hand of the poor). The accuser usurps the advocate's position.
7: בְּהִשָּׁפְטוֹ יֵצֵא רָשָׁע וּתְפִלָּתוֹ תִּהְיֶה לַחֲטָאָה (bĕhishshāpĕṭô yêṣêʾ rāshāʿ ûtĕpillātô tihyeh laḥăṭāʾāh, "When he faces judgment, let him be condemned; let even his prayer be turned against him!") — yêṣêʾ rāshāʿ: "may he come out guilty" (the verdict in the divine court: rāshāʿ as the guilty verdict, as opposed to ṣaddîq/righteous). Ûtĕpillātô tihyeh laḥăṭāʾāh: "and let his prayer become sin" — the most terrifying of all the imprecations: not merely the prayers unanswered but the prayers themselves becoming the source of condemnation. Prayer turned into iniquity is what happens when one who has rejected God's counsel continues to pray as if the relationship is intact (Proverbs 28:9: "if one turns away from hearing the law, even his prayer is an abomination").
8: יִהְיוּ-יָמָיו מְעַטִּים פְּקֻדָּתוֹ יִקַּח אַחֵר (yihyû-yāmāyw mĕʿaṭṭîm pĕquddātô yiqqaḥ ʾaḥêr, "Let his life be cut short; let someone else take his position!") — yimĕʿaṭṭîm: let his days be few (the opposite of the blessing of long life — Psalms 91:16). Pĕquddātô yiqqaḥ ʾaḥêr: "let another take his office/oversight" (pĕquddāh: appointment, office, oversight — from pāqad: to visit, to appoint). This verse is the one Peter quotes in Acts 1:20 of Judas Iscariot: "his office let another take" — the Greek ἐπισκοπή translates pĕquddāh. The apostolic vacancy is filled by citing the Davidic imprecation as prophetic of the betrayer's end and replacement.
9: יִהְיוּ-בָנָיו יְתוֹמִים וְאִשְׁתּוֹ אַלְמָנָה (yihyû-bānāyw yĕtômmîm wĕʾishtô ʾalmānāh, "Let his children be left without a father and his wife become a widow!") — the most socially devastating consequence of a man's death in the ancient world: the widowed wife with no legal protector and the fatherless children exposed to exploitation. The Law specifically protected these groups (Exodus 22:22-24; Deuteronomy 10:18; Psalms 14:29; Psalms 27:19) — their vulnerability made them objects of divine concern. The imprecation turns the enemy into the very social category his own wickedness (v16: "he hounded the poor and needy") exploited.
10: וְנוֹעַ יָנוּעוּ בָנָיו וְשִׁאֵלוּ וְדָרְשׁוּ מֵחָרְבוֹתֵיהֶם (wĕnôaʿ yānûʿû bānāyw wĕshiʾêlû wĕdārĕshû mêḥoorbôtêhem, "Let his children wander and beg, searching for bread far from their ruined homes!") — nôaʿ yānûʿû: the doubled verb intensifies the wandering (the restless, directionless movement of the homeless — as Cain: Genesis 4:12,14: "a fugitive and wanderer"). ḥārbôtêhem: their ruins (the destroyed house from which they must now search for food). The children must beg where the father once had power.
11: יְנַקֵּשׁ נוֹשֶׁה לְכָל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ וְיָבֹזּוּ זָרִים יְגִיעוֹ (yĕnaqqêsh nōsheh lĕkhol-ʾăsher-lô wĕyābōzzû zārîm yĕgîʿô, "Let creditors take everything he owns; let foreigners carry off all he has worked for!") — nōsheh: the creditor (from nāshāh: to lend at interest — Deuteronomy 15:2; Psalms 24:11). The enemy who exploited the poor (v16) now faces the ultimate economic reversal: seized by the creditor, looted by foreigners. Yĕgîʿô: the product of his toil (all the labor-value he accumulated).
12: אַל-יְהִי-לוֹ מֹשֵׁךְ חָסֶד וְאַל-יְהִי חוֹנֵן לִיתוֹמָיו (ʾal-yĕhî-lô mōshêk ḥesed wĕʾal-yĕhî ḥônên lîtômmāyw, "Let no one show him loyal kindness or take pity on the children he left behind!") — the social exclusion: mōshêk ḥesed (one who extends steadfast love — māshak: to draw out, to extend; ḥesed: the covenant loyalty that creates obligation to help). The refusal of ḥesed to the one who showed no ḥesed (v16) is the lex talionis of social obligation. ḥônên lîtômmāyw: one who is gracious to his orphans — even the children receive no grace, because the father's wickedness has exhausted the community's social capital toward his household.
13: יְהִי-אַחֲרִיתוֹ לְהַכְרִית בְּדוֹר אַחֵר יִמַּח שְׁמָם (yĕhî-ʾaḥărîtô lĕhakhrit bĕdôr ʾaḥêr yimmah shĕmām, "Let his line come to an end; let his family name be wiped out in the next generation!") — ʾaḥărît: the latter end/posterity (the future generation that carries a family's name forward). Lĕhakhrit: to be cut off (kārat: the covenantal cutting-off — excision from Israel's community — Leviticus 17:10,14; the most severe covenantal sanction). Yimmah shĕmām: their name will be wiped out (māḥāh: to blot out — Exodus 17:14: "I will utterly blot out the memory of Amalek"; Deuteronomy 9:14; Psalms 25:19). The ultimate fate the Psalmist of v107:43 calls wise people to observe is here invoked against the enemy.
14: יִזָּכֵר עֲוֹן אֲבֹתָיו אֶל-יְהוָה וְחַטַּאת אִמּוֹ אַל-תִּמָּח (yizzākêr ʿăwōn ʾăbōtāyw ʾel-yĕhwāh wĕḥaṭṭat ʾimmô ʾal-timmāḥ, "Let the LORD keep his fathers' guilt on record and never wipe out his mother's sin!") — the covenant principle of generational accountability (Exodus 20:5: "visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children to the third and fourth generation of those who hate me"). The Psalmist asks that the guilty man's family guilt remain on the divine ledger rather than be forgiven. The fathers' ʿāwōn (iniquity) and the mother's ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin/sin-offering) — both formal covenant terms — are to remain unblotted.
15: יִהְיוּ נֶגֶד-יְהוָה תָּמִיד וְיַכְרֵת מֵאֶרֶץ זִכְרָם (yihyû neged-yĕhwāh tāmîd wĕyakhret mêʾereṣ zikhram, "Let all those sins stand always before the LORD, until he wipes out their very memory from the earth!") — neged-yĕhwāh tāmîd: always before the LORD (the divine ledger never cleared — the guilt before the divine gaze without respite of forgiveness). Wĕyakhret mêʾereṣ zikhram: "so that he may cut off their memory from the earth" — zikrām: their remembrance/memorial (the opposite of the enduring name that the righteous receive — Psalms 112:6: "the righteous will be remembered forever"). Memory cut off = the most complete form of judgment.
16: יַעַן אֲשֶׁר לֹא זָכַר עֲשׂוֹת חָסֶד וַיִּרְדֹּף אִישׁ-עָנִי וְאֶבְיוֹן (yaʿan ʾăsher lōʾ zākhar ʿăśôt ḥesed wayyirdōp ʾîsh-ʿānî wĕʾebyôn, "For he never thought to show loyal kindness, but hounded the poor, the needy, and the brokenhearted to the point of killing them!") — lōʾ zākhar ʿăśôt ḥesed: he did not remember to do ḥesed (covenant-loyalty-in-action toward the vulnerable — Micah 6:8: "do justice, love kindness"). Wayyirdōp ʾîsh-ʿānî wĕʾebyôn: he pursued/hounded the poor and needy man. Dākaʾ lêb (in the full verse): the brokenhearted (Psalms 34:18; Psalms 51:17: "a broken and contrite heart, O God, you will not despise"). The irony: he crushed the very people God specifically protects, which is why God specifically crushes him.
17: וַיֶּאֱהַב קְלָלָה וַתְּבוֹאֵהוּ וְלֹא-חָפֵץ בִּבְרָכָה וַתִּרְחַק מִמֶּנּוּ (wayyeʾĕhab qĕlālāh wattĕbôʾêhû wĕlōʾ-ḥāpêṣ bibrākhāh wattirḥaq mimmennu, "He loved to curse — let curses come to him! He took no pleasure in blessing — let blessing stay far from him!") — the principle of moral gravity: what you love finds you. ʾĀhab qĕlālāh: he loved cursing (qālal: the root meaning to make light, to treat as contemptible, to curse). Wĕlōʾ-ḥāpêṣ bibrākhāh: he had no delight in blessing (the word ḥāpêṣ — delight — is the same word used for the LORD's delight in his own steadfast love, Psalms 5:4; Isaiah 53:10). His choices have structured the universe of his reception: cursing-seeking finds cursing; blessing-rejecting cannot receive blessing.
18: וַיִּלְבַּשׁ קְלָלָה כְּמַדּוֹ וַתָּבֹא כַמַּיִם בְּקִרְבּוֹ וְכַשֶּׁמֶן בְּעַצְמוֹתָיו (wayyilbash qĕlālāh kĕmaddô wattābōʾ kammayim bĕqirbô wĕkashshemen bĕʿaṣmôtāyw, "He wrapped himself in curses like a coat; let that curse seep into his body like water and into his bones like oil!") — the garment-of-curses image is uniquely powerful: cursing is not merely something he did but something he clothed himself with, making it his identity-garment. Kammayim bĕqirbô: like water into his inner being (water permeating the body — unstoppable absorption). Kashshemen bĕʿaṣmôtāyw: like oil into his bones (oil penetrating the deepest structural material — the ancient image of deep absorption). The curse, having been his voluntary garment, now soaks into his physiology.
19: תְּהִי-לוֹ כְּבֶגֶד יַעְטֶה וּלְמֵזַח תָּמִיד יַחְגְּרֶהָ (tĕhî-lô kĕbeged yaʿṭeh ûlĕmêzaḥ tāmîd yaḥgĕrehā, "Let it cling to him like the clothing he wears, like a belt strapped on every single day!") — the curse as permanent daily clothing: the garment (beged) pulled on and the belt (mêzaḥ) cinched daily. Tāmîd: always, continually. If he chose cursing as his daily practice and identity, let his identity-garment be the curse itself — inseparable from him as the clothes he wears and the belt that defines his figure. The metaphor of garments recurs in the psalm (v18: the coat of curses; v29: the accusers draped in shame).
20: זֹאת פְּעֻלַּת שֹׂטְנַי מֵאֵת יְהוָה וְהַדֹּבְרִים רָע עַל-נַפְשִׁי (zōʾt pĕʿullat śōṭĕnay mêʾêt yĕhwāh wĕhaddōbĕrîm rāʿ ʿal-napshî, "Let the LORD repay my accusers with all of this — those who speak evil against me!") — śōṭĕnay: my adversaries/accusers (śāṭān turned into a verb-noun for the human adversaries — the same root as v6). This verse returns to the perspective of the Psalmist addressing God about his enemies (plural again — as in vv2-5), suggesting that vv6-19 were indeed the Psalmist's own imprecations. Mêʾêt YHWH: from YHWH (the source of the repayment — not human vengeance but divine justice). The Psalmist hands the verdict to God.
21: וְאַתָּה יְהוִה אֲדֹנָי עֲשֵׂה-אִתִּי לְמַעַן שְׁמֶךָ כִּי-טוֹב חַסְדְּךָ הַצִּילֵנִי (wĕʾattāh YHWH ʾădōnāy ʿăśêh-ʾittî lĕmaʿan shĕmekā kî-ṭôb ḥasdĕkā haṣṣîlênî, "But you, O Lord GOD, deal with me for the sake of your own name; your steadfast love is good — deliver me!") — the great turn: from imprecation to petition. Wĕʾattāh: "but you" — the pivoting contrast (as in Psalms 102:12). The Psalmist does not appeal to his own righteousness (contrast: v20's imprecation rests on the enemies' wickedness) but to the divine character: lĕmaʿan shĕmekā (for the sake of your name — Psalms 23:3; Psalms 25:11; Psalms 31:3; the divine honor at stake in the rescue of the one who bears the divine name). Kî-ṭôb ḥasdĕkā: for your steadfast love is good — the same theology as Psalms 107:1.
22: כִּי-עָנִי וְאֶבְיוֹן אָנֹכִי וְלִבִּי חָלַל בְּקִרְבִּי (kî-ʿānî wĕʾebyôn ʾānōkhî wĕlibbî ḥālal bĕqirbî, "For I am poor and needy, and my heart is deeply wounded within me!") — ʿānî wĕʾebyôn: poor and needy (the classic pair of the socially vulnerable whom the enemy hounded — v16 — and whom God specifically protects — v31). By describing himself as ʿānî wĕʾebyôn, the Psalmist identifies with the very class the enemy victimized, and claims the divine protection specifically promised to them. Ḥālal bĕqirbî: my heart is pierced/wounded within me (ḥālal: to be pierced, to be slain — the word for the mortally wounded — Isaiah 53:5: "he was pierced for our transgressions").
23: כְּצֵל-כִּנְטוֹתוֹ נֶהֱלָכְתִּי נִנְעַרְתִּי כָּאַרְבֶּה (kĕṣêl-kintōṭô nehĕlākhtî ninʿartî kāʾarbeh, "I am fading like an evening shadow, brushed away like a locust!") — kĕṣêl-kintōṭô: like a shadow at its decline (the long evening shadow that fades rapidly — Psalms 102:11; Psalms 144:4). Ninʿartî kāʾarbeh: shaken off like a locust (the locust swept away by the wind or brushed off a person — Nahum 3:17: "like the locust swarm that settles in the hedges on a cold day" and then vanishes when warmed). Both images convey impermanence and the impossibility of holding on.
24: בִּרְכַּי כָּשְׁלוּ מִצּוֹם וּבְשָׂרִי כָּחַשׁ מִשָּׁמֶן (birkay kāshĕlû miṣṣôm ûbĕśārî kāḥash mishshāmen, "My knees buckle from fasting; my body has wasted away, lean and gaunt!") — miṣṣôm: from fasting (the fasting of the Psalmist's prayer life — v4: "I am prayer"). The voluntary fast intensifying intercession has physically depleted him. Kāḥash mishshāmen: become lean/wasted without fat (kāḥash: to grow lean, to be gaunt; mishshāmen: from being oily/full — the fat, prosperous body reduced to emaciation). The intercessor's physical state mirrors his spiritual condition: depleted but not destroyed.
25: וַאֲנִי הָיִיתִי חֶרְפָּה לָהֶם יִרְאוּנִי יְנִיעוּן רֹאשָׁם (waʾănî hāyîtî ḥerpāh lāhem yirʾûnî yĕnîʿûn rōʾshām, "I am the object of their scorn; when they catch sight of me, they shake their heads!") — ḥerpāh: a reproach, the object of public derision (Psalms 22:6: "a reproach of men and despised by the people"). Yirʾûnî yĕnîʿûn rōʾshām: "they see me and wag their heads" — the gesture of contemptuous dismissal (2 Kings 19:21; Lamentations 2:15: the passers-by hissing and shaking their heads at Jerusalem's ruins). Psalms 22:7 uses the same phrase for the righteous sufferer, and Matthew 27:39 uses it for the mockers at the crucifixion — another thread connecting the Davidic sufferer to the Passion.
26: עָזְרֵנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהַי הוֹשִׁיעֵנִי כְחַסְדֶּךָ (ʿāzĕrênî YHWH ʾĕlōhay hôshîʿênî kĕḥasdekā, "Help me, O LORD my God; save me in keeping with your steadfast love!") — the direct, simple petition after the extended description of suffering (vv22-25). ʿĀzar: to help (the fundamental petition for divine assistance — Psalms 121:2: "my help comes from the LORD"). Kĕḥasdekā: according to/in keeping with your steadfast love (the measure of the rescue is the measure of the divine character — not proportional to the Psalmist's merit but to the divine ḥesed's immensity).
27: וְיֵדְעוּ כִּי-יָדְךָ זֹּאת אַתָּה יְהוָה עֲשִׂיתָהּ (wĕyêdĕʿû kî-yādĕkā zōʾt ʾattāh YHWH ʿăśîtāh, "Let them see that your hand is behind this — that you, LORD, are the one who has acted!") — the doxological purpose of the rescue: the witnesses must know (yādaʿ) that this is God's hand, not a natural reversal or a human achievement. Yādĕkā zōʾt: this is your hand (the divine fingerprint on the event — Exodus 14:31: after the sea crossing, Israel saw the great work that the LORD did and feared him). The rescue serves the universal knowledge of God's identity and action.
28: יְקַלְלוּ-הֵמָּה וְאַתָּה תְבָרֵךְ קָמוּ וַיֵּבֹשׁוּ וְעַבְדְּךָ יִשְׂמָח (yĕqallĕlû-hêmmāh wĕʾattāh tĕbārêk qāmû wayyêbōshû wĕʿabdĕkā yiśmāḥ, "Let them curse — you will bless. When they rise against me, let them be shamed; but let your servant be glad!") — the great counterpoint: their cursing vs. God's blessing. The Psalmist does not ask for the curses to stop but for divine blessing to overwhelm them. Qāmû wayyêbōshû: when they arise, let them be shamed (the reversal of the attackers' rise). Wĕʿabdĕkā yiśmāḥ: "but your servant will be glad" — the final state: shame for the attackers, joy for the servant. Romans 12:14 echoes the ethic: "bless those who persecute you; bless and do not curse."
29: יִלְבְּשׁוּ שׂוֹטְנַי כְּלִמָּה וְיַעֲטוּ כַמְעִיל בָּשְׁתָּם (yilbĕshû śōṭĕnay kĕlimmāh wĕyaʿăṭû khameʿîl bāshtām, "Let my accusers be dressed in disgrace and wrapped in their own shame like a mantle!") — the garment-imagery returns (inverting vv18-19): the enemy wrapped himself in curses as a coat; let the enemies be wrapped in shame as a garment. Kĕlimmāh: disgrace, dishonor (the public humiliation of exposure). Kameʿîl bāshtām: their shame as a robe/mantle (meʿîl: the outer garment — the most visible item of clothing). The image is the exact reversal of the royal robe: where honor should be, shame is draped. The psalm's final word on the enemies: clothed in what they deserve.
30: אוֹדֶה יְהוָה מְאֹד בְּפִי וּבְתוֹךְ רַבִּים אֲהַלְלֶנּוּ (ʾôdeh YHWH mĕʾōd bĕpî ûbĕtôk rabbîm ʾăhallennû, "With my voice I will give the LORD great thanks; I will praise him among the gathered crowd!") — the psalm moves from petition and imprecation to vow of praise. ʾôdeh YHWH mĕʾōd: I will thank/praise the LORD greatly (mĕʾōd: exceedingly, greatly — the intensity of the thanksgiving matches the intensity of the suffering described). Bĕtôk rabbîm: in the midst of many (the congregation — Psalms 22:22,25: "I will tell your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will praise you"). Public praise as the proper response to personal rescue.
31: כִּי-יַעֲמֹד לִימִין אֶבְיוֹן לְהוֹשִׁיעַ מִשֹּׁפְטֵי נַפְשׁוֹ (kî-yaʿămōd lîmîn ʾebyôn lĕhôshîaʿ mishshōpĕṭê napshô, "For he stands at the right hand of the poor person, to rescue him from all who would condemn him!") — the closing verse is the great inversion. V6: the enemy placed an accuser at the Psalmist's right hand to condemn him. V31: God stands at the right hand of the ʾebyôn (the poor/needy) to rescue him from those who condemn his soul. The accuser occupies the advocate's position (v6); God occupies the advocate's position (v31). The one who prosecutes is opposed by the divine defender. This verse became the ground for Christian confidence in Christ as advocate (1 John 2:1: "we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous") — standing at the right hand where the accuser tried to stand.
